title: A Hyde Park Larrikin
content:
آ آ آ آ You may have heard of Proclus, sir,
آ آ آ آ If you have been a reader;
آ آ آ آ And you may know a bit of her
آ آ آ آ Who helped the Lycian leader.

آ آ آ آ I have my doubts the head you ï؟½sportï؟½
آ آ آ آ (Now mark me, donï؟½t get crusty)
آ آ آ آ Is hardly of the classic sort
آ آ آ آ Your lore, I think, is fusty.

آ آ آ آ Most likely you have stuck to tracts
آ آ آ آ Flushed through with flaming curses
آ آ آ آ I judge you, neighbour, by your acts
آ آ آ آ So donï؟½t you dآ آ آ آ n my verses.

آ آ آ آ But to my theme. The Asian sage,
آ آ آ آ Whose name above I mention,
آ آ آ آ Lived in the pitchy Pagan age,
آ آ آ آ A life without pretension.

آ آ آ آ He may have worshipped gods like Zeus,
آ آ آ آ And termed old Dis a master;
آ آ آ آ But then he had a strong excuse
آ آ آ آ He never heard a pastor.

آ آ آ آ However, it occurs to me
آ آ آ آ That, had he cut Demeter
آ آ آ آ And followed you, or followed me,
آ آ آ آ He wouldnï؟½t have been sweeter.

آ آ آ آ No doubt with ï؟½shepherdsï؟½ of this time
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s not the ï؟½clean potatoï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Because excuse me for my rhyme
آ آ آ آ He pinned his faith to Plato.

آ آ آ آ But these are facts you canï؟½t deny,
آ آ آ آ My pastor, smudged and sooty,
آ آ آ آ His mind was like a summer sky
آ آ آ آ He lived a life of beauty

آ آ آ آ To lift his brothersï؟½ thoughts above
آ آ آ آ This earth he used to labour:
آ آ آ آ His heart was luminous with love
آ آ آ آ He didnï؟½t wound his neighbour.

آ آ آ آ To him all men were just the same
آ آ آ آ He never foamed at altars,
آ آ آ آ Although he lived ere Moody came
آ آ آ آ Ere Sankey dealt in psalters.

آ آ آ آ The Lycian sage, my ï؟½reverendï؟½ sir,
آ آ آ آ Had not your chances ample;
آ آ آ آ But, after all, I must prefer
آ آ آ آ His perfect, pure example.

آ آ آ آ You, having read the Holy Writ
آ آ آ آ The Book the angels foster
آ آ آ آ Say have you helped us on a bit,
آ آ آ آ You overfed impostor?

آ آ آ آ What have you done to edify,
آ آ آ آ You clammy chapel tinker?
آ آ آ آ What act like his of days gone by
آ آ آ آ The grand old Asian thinker?

آ آ آ آ Is there no deed of yours at all
آ آ آ آ With beauty shining through it?
آ آ آ آ Ah, no! your heart reveals its gall
آ آ آ آ On every side I view it.

آ آ آ آ A blatant bigot with a big
آ آ آ آ Fat heavy fetid carcass,
آ آ آ آ You well become your greasy ï؟½rigï؟½
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½re not a second Arcas.

آ آ آ آ What sort of ï؟½gospelï؟½ do you preach?
آ آ آ آ What ï؟½Bibleï؟½ is your Bible?
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s worse than wormwood in your speech,
آ آ آ آ You livid, living libel!

آ آ آ آ How many lives are growing gray
آ آ آ آ Through your depraved behaviour!
آ آ آ آ I tell you plainly every day
آ آ آ آ You crucify the Saviour!

آ آ آ آ Some evil spirit curses you
آ آ آ آ Your actions never vary:
آ آ آ آ You cannot point your finger to
آ آ آ آ One fact to the contrary.

آ آ آ آ You seem to have a wicked joy
آ آ آ آ In your malicious labour,
آ آ آ آ Endeavouring daily to destroy
آ آ آ آ The neighbourï؟½s love for neighbour.

آ آ آ آ The brutal curses you eject
آ آ آ آ Make strong men dread to hear you.
آ آ آ آ The world outside your petty sect
آ آ آ آ Feels sick when it is near you.

آ آ آ آ No man who shuns that little hole
آ آ آ آ You call your tabernacle
آ آ آ آ Can have, you shriek, a ransomed soul
آ آ آ آ He wears the devilï؟½s shackle.

آ آ آ آ And, hence the ï؟½Papistï؟½ by your clan
آ آ آ آ Is dogged with words inhuman,
آ آ آ آ Because he loves that friend of man
آ آ آ آ The highest type of woman

آ آ آ آ Because he has that faith which sees
آ آ آ آ Before the high Creator
آ آ آ آ A Virgin pleading on her knees
آ آ آ آ A shining Mediator!

آ آ آ آ God help the souls who grope in night
آ آ آ آ Who in your ways have trusted!
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve said enough! the more I write,
آ آ آ آ The more I feel disgusted.

آ آ آ آ The warm, soft air is tainted through
آ آ آ آ With your pernicious leaven.
آ آ آ آ I would not live one hour with you
آ آ آ آ In your peculiar heaven!

آ آ آ آ Now mount your musty pulpit thump,
آ آ آ آ And muddle flat clodhoppers;
آ آ آ آ And let some long-eared booby ï؟½humpï؟½
آ آ آ آ The plate about for coppers.

آ آ آ آ At priest and parson spit and bark,
آ آ آ آ And shake your ï؟½churchï؟½ with curses,
آ آ آ آ You bitter blackguard of the dark
آ آ آ آ With this I close my verses.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Kendall represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Kendall's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
